posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
I’m still trying to figure out which champagnes will last until I’m 101 years old. And why.
Grapes, Acid, dosage?

I’d be interested to hear what folks think will be the modern champagnes that will age for generations if any?

Oldest Ive had was a 1993 Dom and it was glorious.
I feel that was probably a freak show though.
Ancient champagne…(does most just go sour?)

posts.name: Brig Campbell
posts.cooked: I’m going with CO2 which is basically an insert gas.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Low oxygen transfer and cold storage. But even with all that, you still lose some bubbles and freshness. But the original release 1969 DP was glorious last week.

posts.name: Zachary Pearson
posts.cooked: Dosage and high acid. I wonder how the modern trend for Extra Brut/no dosage is going to play out over the next 15-20 years.

posts.name: Tom Won
posts.cooked:
I think bone dry Champagnes wont age very well because even ripe fruits tend to fade after decades of storage.

On top of my head, I’m thinking bottles like LP Grand Siecle and Billecart-Salmon cuvee Nicolas Francois to be age-worthy, although I’m also curious what those demi-secs will be like after 30+ years(Moet ice anyone?).

posts.name: Nikita Malhotra
posts.cooked: I have also been wondering how extra brut and zero dosage will play out in terms of aging. I will have to be mindful of making notes in the coming years…

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
NMalhotra:

I have also been wondering how extra brut and zero dosage will play out in terms of aging. I will have to be mindful of making notes in the coming years…

It’s a really interesting question; I’ve asked a couple of winemakers who make wine in this style and they’ve suggested that they’re less worried about this issue because dosage was used in to balance the difficulty of getting sufficiently ripe fruit in Champagne. (Not saying this is necessarily true.) Ultimately will be one for other people to really get into, as I don’t love my Champagne with that much age (i.e. more than 35 years) anyway. But there are a few big houses I’m really curious about, especially since Roederer now makes a Brut Nature.

posts.cooked:
When did there first start to be notable 0-3 g/L type Champagnes?

I’m quite curious about the aging too. It would be interesting to see recent notes about whatever were the earliest good bottlings like that.

Maybe the relative aging of traditonal German Riesling versus GG might offer some suggestion. Having said that, I don’t know how well that is understood yet either.

posts.cooked: BTW, that’s a summary of my experiences. I frankly see a non-dosage product and don’t buy them. I really only have them at tastings, and I find them entirely too austere (for the most part), so there is a total lack of enjoyment for me with the category.

posts.name: Greg Kahn

Ian_Dorin:

BTW, that’s a summary of my experiences. I frankly see a non-dosage product and don’t buy them. I really only have them at tastings, and I find them entirely too austere (for the most part), so there is a total lack of enjoyment for me with the category.

There’s a range, too - Laval’s non dosage tastes quite different to Bouchard’s and then again to Lassaigne’s Clos St. Sophie. And then, what about 1g vs 0? Etc. etc. It’s a really interesting question and I don’t remotely claim to have a good answer. A number of growers regularly come in around 2gs - is that closer to the traditional amount of dosage or closer to nature?

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
Well. What do we mean by age?

You won’t get a lot of Maillard without sugar. But there’s more to champagne than that.

Do all low dosage wines fall over and die within five years? Of course not.

I’ve endless Lilbert, Peters, Tarlant, Boulard, Diebolt-Vallois, Gimmonet, Launois, Beaufort and Selosse from the early naughties that have aged very well. A few that haven’t.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
Yup, right there with you.

Laval and Bouchard tend to be a bit riper though, hence the more vinous style of both. Other than that, I’ve never studied the grams

posts.name: Jordan_Rock
posts.cooked:
I’ve been wondering the same thing lately, especially how grower Champagne that tends to use 0 - 3g dosage age vs. traditional big house Champagne that seems to be glorious even after 25 - 50+ years.

I don’t buy supposed argument that there’s not much difference because dosage was/is used to compensate for less ripe fruit – ripe fruit in Champagne doesn’t equal RS like dosage does.

Do we know how much dosage we need to notice Maillard reaction with age? Does anyone know how 0 dosage BdB, for example, ages vs. still Chardonnay?

I’ll ask the producers I visit on my upcoming visit to the region, but I wonder how many actually know and how many answers will be heavily shaped by bias.

posts.name: Mark Ye
posts.cooked:
Russell_Faulkner:

Peters, Tarlant, Boulard, Diebolt-Vallois, Gimmonet, Launois, Beaufort and Selosse from the early naughties that have aged very well.

They are naughty by nature.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked: what were dosage levels in, say, the 1940s etc? much higher?

posts.name: Brig Campbell
posts.cooked:
ybarselah:

what were dosage levels in, say, the 1940s etc? much higher?

In France and in other countries, it is slowly, after 1920s, that champagne dosage gave way to brut as we know it today. The proportion of brut champagne reached half of the total shipments at the beginning of the 1950s and kept growing to between 85 to 90% today. A brut champagne has a dosage between 0 to 12 grams of sugar per liter. Champagne’s ten largest brut non-vintage brands have dropped their dosages since 1991 nearly by 2.8g/l, and are all under 11g/l today, with the average at 9g/l. Brut Imperial, the most selling Brut Multi Vintage, from Moet & Chandon lowered its dosage from 12g to 9g.

champagneeveryday – 13 Nov 20

The Changing Taste of Champagne: the Role of Dosage

Words by Jean-Remi Barbier reasons of this change and how important the impact of dosage is.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
But this is a myth IMHO.

Which big house champagnes from 88-04 are consistently great today? Krug is variable to say the least, Goisses of that era are patchy, DP has quite a few misses. Pol Roger poor in the 90s. Which 96s are holding well today? Magnums make the situation better.

It’s probably true that the 90s were a bad patch and things will be different in a few years for 20-30 year old grand marques. Let’s see.

But back to then original question of how low dosage grower wines might age, a valid answer would be who cares? Young and old champagnes are basically different drinks and despite having a cellar with plenty of DP, Krug, Comtes etc. I more often than not reach for a young wine.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
brigcampbell:

I’m going with CO2 which is basically an insert gas.

Nope. I’ve had many oxidized Champagnes that have still retained their CO2. Oxygen can get inside bottle without CO2 getting out.

Basically things like pH and/or acidity, the level of dry extract and fruit concentration are things that make a Champagne age well.

Dosage doesn’t help a Champagne to age in itself, it helps Champagne to develop those toasty / caramelized maillard reaction notes people associate with an aged Champagne. Champagnes without any RS can still age wonderfully, they just take another route. (But I myself prefer toasty Champagnes, so I’d rather age Brut than Brut Nature Champagnes, all other things being equal).

posts.name: Eric Egan
posts.cooked: Wine making style is surely also important. I’ve had quite a lot of very old champagnes that were amazing (though of course a completely different drink than a young champagne), whether they have retained their bubbles or not. By far the best have come from producers that were known for a more oxidative style of winemaking, like Bollinger or Krug from the 60s and older. I even had a bunch of half full half bottles of Bollinger 1962 that were all outstanding… maderized or course and had very little in common with a fruity young champagne (or an oxidative one for that matter), but an outstanding drink nonetheless.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
It isn’t just Maillard-like reactions, it’s also the degradation of sugars to furfurals. I think the latter is more important for the really caramelized flavors you get in e.g. old Moët.

Different sugars (cane, beet, MCR) behave quite differently, too.

And ripe fruit in Champagne can certainly lead to RS that isn’t from dosage. There are some zero dosage Champagnes that have 5-10 grams of residual sugar when you analyze them…

In short, it’s a complex question!

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: One grower made a demi-sec zero dosage wine. I am not sure it wasnt a mistake though!

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

And ripe fruit in Champagne can certainly lead to RS that isn’t from dosage. There are some zero dosage Champagnes that have 5-10 grams of residual sugar when you analyze them…

I’ve always wondered the relationship between low/zero dosage and the ripeness of the harvested grapes (plus maybe residual sugar after fermentation).

Broadly speaking, do grapes harvested for low/zero dosage Champagne tend to have higher Brix than grapes harvested for Champagne made in a more traditional Brut style?

And if so, which is the cause and which is the effect? I.e. does the producer decide to make a low/zero Champagne and thus use sites and/or hang time decisions to get riper grapes? Or does the producer have sites that achieve higher ripeness and therefore decide to use less dosage?

I’m sure there isn’t a single answer to those questions, but I wonder if anyone in the know could generalize.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
J_Rock:

I’ll ask the producers I visit on my upcoming visit to the region, but I wonder how many actually know and how many answers will be heavily shaped by bias.

This is why I want to talk to Jean-Baptiste, who now has 25 years experience running Roederer’s cave and makes both a traditionally does tete de cuvee and a zero dosage bottling. I’m less concerned by bias, but I think a lot of growers simply won’t have the experience to answer such a broad question. Agrapart might be another one, he certainly makes some interesting cuvees - the Experience, can be…an experience.

posts.name: Warren Taranow
posts.cooked:
Chris_Seiber:

When did there first start to be notable 0-3 g/L type Champagnes?

René Collard was releasing low- and non-dosage champagne in the eighties. A trailblazer, he was also releasing some Pinot Meunier bottlings. I don’t know of anyone earlier, but I’d suspect there were some during atypically ripe vintages.
